news_summary: Progress in human rights is advancing as Venezuela explores universal jurisdiction to ensure accountability and justice.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès sont réalisés en matière de droits de l'homme alors que le Venezuela explore la question de la compétence universelle afin de garantir la responsabilité et la justice.
news_summary_es: Se están logrando avances en materia de derechos humanos a medida que Venezuela estudia la jurisdicción universal para garantizar la rendición de cuentas y la justicia.

articles.title: Venezuela: Universal Jurisdiction a Route to Justice
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Anti-government students show a national flag to a National Guard barricade during a protest in Caracas, Venezuela, on February 16, 2014.
                    © 2014 Leo Ramirez/AFP via Getty Images
          
    


  
(Madrid) – Argentina’s efforts to extradite and question a former Venezuelan official residing in Spain accused of crimes against humanity could offer a pathway toward accountability, Human Rights Watch said today, following Spanish authorities’ reported decision to continue proceedings on the extradition request.Argentina seeks the extradition of Ephraín Enrique Verdú Torrelles, a former officer of the Venezuelan Bolivarian National Guard (Guardia Nacional Bolivariana, GNB), a militarized police force responsible for widespread human rights violations. The extradition request seeks to secure Verdú Torrelles’ presence for questioning in connection with allegations that he committed murder as a crime against humanity during a government crackdown on the 2014 protests in Venezuela. “Victims in Venezuela have seen no justice at home, and Argentina’s request for extradition is a reminder that justice can cross borders,” said Michelle Reyes Milk, senior international justice counsel at Human Rights Watch. “It will now be up to the Spanish court to decide on whether to proceed with extradition.” The Spanish Council of Ministers approved the continuation of the proceedings on the extradition request, referring the matter to the Audiencia Nacional (Spain’s national court). The representative of the Venezuelan victims, Ignacio Jovtis, told Human Rights Watch that Spanish authorities have notified Verdú Torrelles about the proceedings on the extradition request. During the 2014 protests in Venezuela, security forces systematically used excessive and unlawful force against largely peaceful demonstrators, arbitrarily arresting hundreds and subjecting many detainees to severe abuse including beatings and, at times, torture, while denying them due process. Security forces and pro-government gangs known as colectivos also committed extrajudicial killings. The United Nations Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Venezuela found reasonable grounds to believe that government violations amounted to crimes against humanity, and that the government’s deliberate weakening of judicial mechanisms in the country worsened impunity. In 2021, the International Criminal Court opened an investigation into alleged crimes against humanity in Venezuela. The extradition request follows a criminal complaint filed in June 2023 by the team of InterJust, a human rights group, before Argentinian courts, on behalf of family members of individuals alleged to have been killed extrajudicially by members of the Bolivarian National Guard during the 2014 protests. This case is possible due to the principle of universal jurisdiction, which allows countries to investigate and prosecute certain grave human rights violations amounting to serious international crimes, regardless of where they were committed or the nationality of the suspects or victims. A separate and parallel complaint was lodged by an Argentinian human rights group, Foro Argentino para la Defensa de la Democracia (Argentine Forum for the Defense of Democracy), in January 2023, against former Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro and his interior minister, Diosdado Cabello. Maduro has been detained in US federal custody since January 3 and is awaiting trial in the Southern District of New York on narco‑terrorism, drug trafficking, and weapons charges. Cabello continues to serve as minister of interior, justice and peace in Venezuela. He also faces narco-terrorism, drug trafficking, and weapons charges in the United States. In September 2024, the investigating judge in Argentina issued arrest warrants against Verdú Torrelles and 13 other GNB officers to question them about the case filed against them. Under applicable Argentinian criminal procedure, Verdú Torrelles and the other officers have not been charged with a crime at this stage. Instead, Verdú Torrelles has been formally notified that he is the subject of an ongoing criminal investigation, procedurally becoming an imputado. Separately, the judge also summoned and issued arrest warrants against Maduro and Cabello.If extradited, Verdú Torrelles would become the first individual in this case to appear in person before the Argentinian courts. Extradition would allow the investigation against him to move forward procedurally in Argentina, including questioning and possible indictment, after which the case could be sent to trial. This would make Verdú Torrelles, to date, the highest-level individual to face a court anywhere for grave human rights violations committed in Venezuela, Human Rights Watch said. Ongoing universal jurisdiction cases in Argentina concern alleged crimes under international law in Myanmar, Colombia, Nicaragua, China, Israel and Palestine, and Franco-era Spain. These cases have faced challenges, including securing the presence of suspects on Argentinian territory, the lack of specialized investigative and prosecutorial units with the mandate to conduct universal jurisdiction cases, and other resource limitations. At the same time, Argentinian judges and prosecutors have prosecuted important cases regarding international crimes committed in Argentina in the past. “Argentina’s pursuit of universal jurisdiction cases sends a clear and resounding message that there should be no safe haven for those responsible for grave human rights violations,” said Reyes Milk. “The cooperation of other states, like Spain, is key to that effort.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Anti-government students show a national flag to a National Guard barricade during a protest in Caracas, Venezuela, on February 16, 2014. © 2014 Leo Ramirez/AFP via Getty Images

(Madrid) – Argentina’s efforts to extradite and question a former Venezuelan official residing in Spain accused of crimes against humanity could offer a pathway toward accountability, Human Rights Watch said today, following Spanish authorities’ reported decision to continue proceedings on the extradition request.

Argentina seeks the extradition of Ephraín Enrique Verdú Torrelles, a former officer of the Venezuelan Bolivarian National Guard (Guardia Nacional Bolivariana, GNB), a militarized police force responsible for widespread human rights violations. The extradition request seeks to secure Verdú Torrelles’ presence for questioning in connection with allegations that he committed murder as a crime against humanity during a government crackdown on the 2014 protests in Venezuela.

“Victims in Venezuela have seen no justice at home, and Argentina’s request for extradition is a reminder that justice can cross borders,” said Michelle Reyes Milk, senior international justice counsel at Human Rights Watch. “It will now be up to the Spanish court to decide on whether to proceed with extradition.”

The Spanish Council of Ministers approved the continuation of the proceedings on the extradition request, referring the matter to the Audiencia Nacional (Spain’s national court). The representative of the Venezuelan victims, Ignacio Jovtis, told Human Rights Watch that Spanish authorities have notified Verdú Torrelles about the proceedings on the extradition request. During the 2014 protests in Venezuela, security forces systematically used excessive and unlawful force against largely peaceful demonstrators, arbitrarily arresting hundreds and subjecting many detainees to severe abuse including beatings and, at times, torture, while denying them due process. Security forces and pro-government gangs known as colectivos also committed extrajudicial killings.

The United Nations Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Venezuela found reasonable grounds to believe that government violations amounted to crimes against humanity, and that the government’s deliberate weakening of judicial mechanisms in the country worsened impunity. In 2021, the International Criminal Court opened an investigation into alleged crimes against humanity in Venezuela.

The extradition request follows a criminal complaint filed in June 2023 by the team of InterJust, a human rights group, before Argentinian courts, on behalf of family members of individuals alleged to have been killed extrajudicially by members of the Bolivarian National Guard during the 2014 protests. This case is possible due to the principle of universal jurisdiction, which allows countries to investigate and prosecute certain grave human rights violations amounting to serious international crimes, regardless of where they were committed or the nationality of the suspects or victims.

A separate and parallel complaint was lodged by an Argentinian human rights group, Foro Argentino para la Defensa de la Democracia (Argentine Forum for the Defense of Democracy), in January 2023, against former Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro and his interior minister, Diosdado Cabello. Maduro has been detained in US federal custody since January 3 and is awaiting trial in the Southern District of New York on narco‑terrorism, drug trafficking, and weapons charges. Cabello continues to serve as minister of interior, justice and peace in Venezuela. He also faces narco-terrorism, drug trafficking, and weapons charges in the United States.

In September 2024, the investigating judge in Argentina issued arrest warrants against Verdú Torrelles and 13 other GNB officers to question them about the case filed against them. Under applicable Argentinian criminal procedure, Verdú Torrelles and the other officers have not been charged with a crime at this stage. Instead, Verdú Torrelles has been formally notified that he is the subject of an ongoing criminal investigation, procedurally becoming an imputado. Separately, the judge also summoned and issued arrest warrants against Maduro and Cabello.

If extradited, Verdú Torrelles would become the first individual in this case to appear in person before the Argentinian courts. Extradition would allow the investigation against him to move forward procedurally in Argentina, including questioning and possible indictment, after which the case could be sent to trial.

This would make Verdú Torrelles, to date, the highest-level individual to face a court anywhere for grave human rights violations committed in Venezuela, Human Rights Watch said.

Ongoing universal jurisdiction cases in Argentina concern alleged crimes under international law in Myanmar, Colombia, Nicaragua, China, Israel and Palestine, and Franco-era Spain. These cases have faced challenges, including securing the presence of suspects on Argentinian territory, the lack of specialized investigative and prosecutorial units with the mandate to conduct universal jurisdiction cases, and other resource limitations. At the same time, Argentinian judges and prosecutors have prosecuted important cases regarding international crimes committed in Argentina in the past.

“Argentina’s pursuit of universal jurisdiction cases sends a clear and resounding message that there should be no safe haven for those responsible for grave human rights violations,” said Reyes Milk. “The cooperation of other states, like Spain, is key to that effort.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Argentina's use of universal jurisdiction to pursue justice for serious human rights violations committed in Venezuela, representing a significant step toward accountability for crimes against humanity. This has broad significance for victims and international justice, with detailed context on legal proceedings and cooperation between countries.
articles.personality_title: Argentina seeks extradition of Venezuelan officer accused of crimes against humanity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 2014, Venezuela saw large protests where security forces used violence against peaceful demonstrators. Many people were arrested, abused, or killed without fair trials. The country’s courts have not held those responsible accountable.

**What happened** – Argentina is trying to bring Ephraín Enrique Verdú Torrelles, a former Venezuelan National Guard officer living in Spain, to face questioning for his alleged role in murders during the 2014 protests. Spain has agreed to continue the legal process to decide if Verdú Torrelles should be extradited to Argentina. This request is based on the principle of universal jurisdiction, which lets countries investigate serious crimes even if they happened elsewhere.

**Impact** – If extradited, Verdú Torrelles would be the highest-ranking Venezuelan official to face court for crimes against humanity. This case shows how countries can work together to seek justice when local courts fail. It also highlights the use of international law to hold people accountable for human rights abuses, offering hope to victims who have not seen justice in Venezuela.

**What's next step** – The Spanish court will decide whether to send Verdú Torrelles to Argentina. If he is extradited, Argentina will question him and could formally charge him, moving the case closer to trial. This process may encourage other countries to take similar actions for serious crimes committed around the world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Argentina’s pursuit of a Venezuelan officer accused of crimes against humanity marks a key step in using international law to bring justice across borders.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Argentine demande l’extradition d’un officier vénézuélien accusé de crimes contre l’humanité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 2014, le Venezuela a connu de grandes manifestations où les forces de sécurité ont utilisé la violence contre des manifestants pacifiques. Beaucoup de personnes ont été arrêtées, maltraitées ou tuées sans procès équitable. Les tribunaux du pays n’ont pas tenu les responsables pour compte.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Argentine tente de faire venir Ephraín Enrique Verdú Torrelles, un ancien officier de la Garde Nationale vénézuélienne vivant en Espagne, pour l’interroger sur son rôle présumé dans des meurtres lors des manifestations de 2014. L’Espagne a accepté de poursuivre la procédure pour décider si Verdú Torrelles doit être extradé vers l’Argentine. Cette demande repose sur le principe de juridiction universelle, qui permet aux pays d’enquêter sur des crimes graves même s’ils ont eu lieu ailleurs.

**Impact** – S’il est extradé, Verdú Torrelles serait le plus haut responsable vénézuélien à comparaître devant un tribunal pour crimes contre l’humanité. Cette affaire montre comment les pays peuvent coopérer pour rechercher la justice quand les tribunaux locaux échouent. Elle met aussi en lumière l’usage du droit international pour tenir les auteurs de violations des droits humains responsables, offrant un espoir aux victimes qui n’ont pas vu justice au Venezuela.

**Prochaine étape** – Le tribunal espagnol décidera s’il envoie Verdú Torrelles en Argentine. S’il est extradé, l’Argentine l’interrogera et pourra le poursuivre officiellement, rapprochant l’affaire d’un procès. Ce processus pourrait encourager d’autres pays à agir de même pour des crimes graves commis dans le monde.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La démarche de l’Argentine contre un officier vénézuélien accusé de crimes contre l’humanité est une étape clé pour faire respecter la justice internationale au-delà des frontières.
articles.personality_title_es: Argentina busca la extradición de un oficial venezolano acusado de crímenes de lesa humanidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 2014, Venezuela vivió grandes protestas donde las fuerzas de seguridad usaron violencia contra manifestantes pacíficos. Muchas personas fueron arrestadas, maltratadas o asesinadas sin juicios justos. Los tribunales del país no han responsabilizado a los culpables.

**Qué pasó** – Argentina intenta traer a Ephraín Enrique Verdú Torrelles, un exoficial de la Guardia Nacional Venezolana que vive en España, para que responda por su presunta participación en asesinatos durante las protestas de 2014. España ha aceptado continuar con el proceso legal para decidir si Verdú Torrelles debe ser extraditado. Esta solicitud se basa en el principio de jurisdicción universal, que permite a los países investigar crímenes graves aunque hayan ocurrido en otro lugar.

**Impacto** – Si es extraditado, Verdú Torrelles sería el funcionario venezolano de más alto rango en enfrentar un juicio por crímenes de lesa humanidad. Este caso muestra cómo los países pueden cooperar para buscar justicia cuando los tribunales locales no actúan. También destaca el uso del derecho internacional para responsabilizar a los autores de violaciones a los derechos humanos, dando esperanza a las víctimas que no han visto justicia en Venezuela.

**Próximo paso** – El tribunal español decidirá si envía a Verdú Torrelles a Argentina. Si es extraditado, Argentina lo interrogará y podría acusarlo formalmente, acercando el caso a un juicio. Este proceso podría motivar a otros países a tomar acciones similares por crímenes graves en todo el mundo.

**Frase resumen** – La búsqueda de Argentina para extraditar a un oficial venezolano acusado de crímenes de lesa humanidad es un paso clave para hacer cumplir la justicia internacional más allá de las fronteras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a balanced scale held gently by two hands emerging from a map blending the shapes of Venezuela, Argentina, and Spain, with one side of the scale holding a broken chain symbolizing justice breaking through impunity, set against a soft, warm background of muted earth tones and natural blues.
